name: Brownie
description: A Brownie is a household spirit from Scottish folklore, said to inhabit homes and assist with chores while the family sleeps. They are generally benevolent creatures, though easily offended and known to abandon a house if mistreated. Brownies are typically described as small, wizened men with brown skin and hair, often unclothed or wearing rags. They are said to be solitary creatures, each attached to a specific household. Their work includes tasks such as sweeping, dusting, churning butter, and grinding grain. In return for their services, Brownies typically request only small offerings of food, such as porridge or milk. Attempts to pay them more generously are often met with displeasure, leading them to depart. The Brownie's connection to a specific location often results in tales of them moving with a family, or remaining in a place long after the original inhabitants have gone, forever tied to the land and the memory of those who once lived there.
subtitle: A helpful household spirit from Scottish folklore.
appearance: Small, wizened humanoids with brown skin and hair, often unclothed or wearing rags. They are sometimes described as having wrinkled faces and being no more than a few feet tall.
habitat: Private homes, barns, and other domestic spaces, primarily in rural areas of Scotland and England.
behavior: Secretive and industrious, working at night while the household sleeps. They are generally helpful but easily offended by perceived slights or attempts to pay them directly. They are known to be territorial and protective of their homes.

keyMyths.title: The Brownie of Bodsbeck
keyMyths.description: The Brownie of Bodsbeck is a famous tale about a Brownie who served a Covenanter family during times of persecution. He performed many tasks, even warning them of approaching enemies. However, when the family attempted to provide him with a new coat, he took offense and left. The story highlights the Brownie's aversion to direct payment and their dedication to service for simple offerings.
keyMyths.source: The Brownie of Bodsbeck, James Hogg

keyMyths.title: The Silken Gown
keyMyths.description: This story recounts a time when a family, intending to show gratitude to their Brownie, left him a beautiful silken gown. The Brownie, upon finding the gown, lamented his changed status and declared he would no longer work for the family, as he was now a 'gentleman'. This tale illustrates the Brownie's preference for simple, unacknowledged service over elaborate gifts.
keyMyths.source: Folk Tales of Scotland, various sources

keyMyths.title: The Grinding Quern
keyMyths.description: A Brownie was once employed to turn a grinding quern, providing flour for a household. He worked tirelessly, but the family, eager to reward him, left out a bowl of cream and a piece of cake. Offended by the direct payment, the Brownie smashed the quern and disappeared, leaving the family without his valuable service. This shows the Brownie's independent nature and dislike for being treated as an employee.
